sections.heading: Epidemiology and Risk Factors
sections.content: Soft tissue sarcomas are a rare and heterogeneous group of solid tumors of mesenchymal origin, accounting for only 1% of all adult malignancies and 15% of childhood malignancies. They can be broadly divided into sarcomas of soft tissues (including fat, muscle, nerve and nerve sheath, blood vessels, and other connective tissues) and sarcomas of bone. The anatomic site of the primary disease represents an important variable that influences treatment and outcome. Extremities (43%), the trunk (10%), visceral (19%), retroperitoneum (15%), or head and neck (9%) are the most common primary sites. Tumors arising in the abdominal cavity more commonly metastasize to the liver and peritoneum, while most other soft tissue sarcomas metastasize to the lungs. The true incidence is underestimated, especially because gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) may not have been included in registry databases before 2001. Prior radiation therapy (RT) is a known risk factor, as are various chemicals and genetic syndromes.

sections.heading: Histologic Classification and Diagnostic Evaluation
sections.content: More than 50 different histologic subtypes have been identified. Pathologic assessment is critical, and morphologic diagnosis based on microscopic examination remains the gold standard. However, ancillary techniques like immunohistochemistry, cytogenetics, and molecular genetic testing are essential for accurate diagnosis and classification, as many subtypes are defined by specific genetic alterations. The 2020 World Health Organization classification organizes these tumors into distinct categories based on line of differentiation (e.g., adipocytic, fibroblastic/myofibroblastic, smooth muscle, skeletal muscle, vascular, peripheral nerve sheath, tumors of uncertain differentiation).

sections.heading: Staging and Treatment
sections.content: Staging uses the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition, which incorporates TNM classification, tumor size, histologic grade (using the FNCLCC system), and depth. Multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment by a team with sarcoma expertise is mandatory for optimal management.
